data.title: HFRO: Controversial Fund, But May Be Too Cheap With A Massive Discount To NAV
data.description:
HFRO Fund History

Highland Opportunities and Income Fund (NYSE:HFRO) is a controversial closed end fund that invests primarily in real estate assets but also holds a smattering of equity, CLOs, fixed and floating rate loans.

It has been about eighteen months since my last article on HFRO.

At the time, I rated HFRO a Hold and recommended a small starter position when it was at a 37% discount and adding more shares if the discount went above 40%. With the benefit of hindsight, it would have been much better to wait until later in 2024 after a surprise 50% distribution cut in January 2024 caused a mass exit from dividend oriented investors.

Overall, the HFRO NAV and market performance has been pretty dismal since late 2023, but there have been some trading opportunities since then to help alleviate the damage. There was a tender offer earlier this year, where you could have exchanged about 46% of your shares for preferred stock.

I decided to write an update now, because I believe the HFRO management may soon be initiating a new share repurchase program. With a 57% discount to NAV, such a program appears to be a no-brainer.

For quite some time now, HFRO has been trading with a discount well above 50% which is the highest discount in the US closed-end fund universe tracked on cefconnect.com.

Why is the Discount So High?

  • Very High Exposure To Illiquid Assets: In the last holdings reports to shareholders as of March 31, 2025. HFRO included the following blurb in a small footnote:

    • “Securities with a total aggregate value of $757,415,160, or 87.4% of net assets, were classified as Level 3 within the three-tier fair value hierarchy”.

    • Note that a significant portion of the Level 3 assets are secured by real estate which is more difficult to value accurately than publicly traded stocks or bonds.

    • Many investors are especially concerned in this case because several of the Level 3 illiquid assets are controlled or related to the NexPoint and Highland management companies. Management benefits from higher management fees if the NAV fair value marks to market are too high.

  • Distribution Cut: The discount widened a lot after a large 50% distribution cut at the end of 2023, and has never really recovered. A higher distribution returns more capital to shareholders allowing them to recover more of the discount. The distribution cut rubbed a lot of income investors the wrong way and they likely dumped their HFRO shares.

  • Highly Complex Portfolio: HFRO holds a widely diverse set of assets which may be hard to understand. Management does do a pretty good job in a quarterly shareholder report of describing the top ten holdings in some detail, but the diversity of assets in both real estate and non-real estate can be difficult to value.

  • Shareholder Concerns About Persistent Discount: The fund management has taken some limited actions to reduce the discount, but has not been aggressive enough in returning capital to shareholders.

    • Two years ago, management initiated a $100 million share buyback program. But only about $20 million of this program was actually utilized, and the buyback program has just ended. I recently sent a message to Shareholder Relations asking if they will be extending the share buyback program going forward.

    • The fund instituted a $100 million tender offer for shareholders this year offering $10 a share. But the $10 a share was not paid in cash and no portfolio assets had to be liquidated. Instead they issued $100 million of a new 5.375% preferred stock (HFRO.PR.B). By doing this, the fund management did not have to return any cash to shareholders, so the management fee which is based on assets under management remained the same. When the new preferred came out, the market price traded around $16, so the effective price that investors could have realized was about $6.40 a share, not $10.00.

  • The “Dondero” Discount: Two closed-end funds run by Highland Capital Management, (NYSE:HFRO) and (HGLB) tend to have high persistent discounts to NAV. There has been a lot of litigation involved in companies controlled by James Dondero. For example Highland Capital Management, an investment advisor controlled by Dondero, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection in October, 2019. While this did not directly hurt shareholders in HFRO or HGLB, such events can erode investor confidence.

The recent market performance of HFRO has been mixed, partially because swings in the discount have a much larger effect than the actual NAV performance. More active closed-end fund traders may have benefited from the recent $10 tender offer with a proration of about 46%. It would have been possible to sell off their preferred shares above $16.00 (equivalent to a common share price of $6.40) the first few weeks after the tender offer closed.

Some Recent Developments

1) Since the distribution cut at the end of 2023, the fund has paid out regular monthly distributions of $0.0385. Management has not discussed raising the distribution again. I suspect they would prefer share buybacks which gives them more flexibility in how much is returned to shareholders.

2) The Credit Suisse case, which seems to drag on forever, is back in the Texas fifth circuit court of appeals. The Fund has provided a link for any shareholder who wants to follow the Credit Suisse litigation in detail- TJB | 5th COA The court number is 05-24-01124-CV.

If there are any attorneys reading this article, perhaps they can comment on this case. As a non-attorney, I felt that it is quite possible that HFRO may not receive anything additional, or perhaps only a small amount. Perhaps a compromise will be reached to reduce the legal fees for both sides.

3) The two year $100 million share buyback expired in May 2025. I have sent a note to Investor Relations asking/suggesting that a new share repurchase agreement should be initiated since the discount to NAV is well above 50%.

Some investors would likely feel this is a waste of my time. But I was surprised back in 2023. Here is an excerpt from an old HFRO article I wrote back in November, 2023:

“There were no share repurchases in the second quarter of 2023. I contacted Investor Relations and asked them if they would consider providing more frequent updates of their share repurchase activities. This is done by several other closed-end funds. For example, SPE provides monthly updates of their share buyback activity.

They told me that for now, they only plan to provide updates in the annual and semi-annual shareholder reports. Perhaps if other shareholders request more frequent share buyback updates, the Fund's Board will reconsider this policy.”

I was pleasantly surprised that HFRO did start to report the share repurchase activity in 2024 on a quarterly basis. I would have preferred monthly reporting, similar to SPE, but the quarterly updates are a big improvement over what we had before.

Three Year Discount History for HFRO

Here is the asset allocation breakdown as of March 31, 2025:

Portfolio Allocation Breakdown

Number of Holdings= 72

Leverage Percentage= 26.4%

Source: HFRO Fund Fact Sheet Q1 2025

Real estate is the largest allocation category by far. A more detailed summary of the real estate holdings was presented in the last shareholder presentation. The largest allocations are to Multifamily, Self Storage, Single family Rentals and Life Sciences with little or no allocation to the troubled retail and commercial office sectors.

Portfolio - Top 10 Exposures (as of March 31, 2025)

I know that many investors are highly suspect of the NAV reported by HFRO. But I believe that the NAV reported by HFRO management is probably pretty close to being accurate. A precise valuation of any private investment is always difficult and adds a lot of uncertainty to the NAV.

Keep in mind that the NAV uncertainty can occur in both directions, so it is even possible that The NAV could be understated depending on how things play out in the future with the economy.

The current 57% discount seems more than adequate to account for the added NAV uncertainty, especially if management re-enacts the share repurchase program. Of course this is only true if management actually starts to aggressively buy back shares in the open market.

Trading Opportunities in HFRO

I have owned a core position in HFRO for some time now. Because of big swings in its discount to NAV, there are sometimes opportunities to add shares on a sharp downward move in the price, or to trim shares when the stock bounces up rapidly.

The $100 million tender offer earlier this year also provided some good opportunities, especially for those who aggressively sold the HFRO-B preferred when it first started to trade. I did trim a few of the HFRO-B shares from the tender offer north of $16, but in hindsight I was not nearly aggressive enough. With the preferred trading closer to $15, I am holding my remaining shares and would look to add more shares on dips below $15.

Fund Performance

Here is the year-by-year total return performance record since 2018 when the fund converted to the closed-end fund format. Performance has been poor on both an absolute and relative basis in 2023 and 2024, after a good year in 2022.

Morningstar uses the Bank Loan category for HFRO, but they should probably move HFRO to one of the real estate categories, since the fund has changed considerably over the past few years. The performance numbers do not take into account any gains an investor may have made from the tender offer in 2024.

Alpha is Generated by High Discount + High Distributions

When you invest in HFRO, there is a possibility of capturing some "alpha" from the high discount and distribution yield.

The distribution rate of 9.13% along with the 57.6% discount allows investors to capture some alpha by recovering a small portion of the NAV whenever a monthly distribution is paid out.

When you recover NAV from a fund selling at a 57.6% discount, the percentage gain is 1.00/ 0.424 or about 135.8%. So the annual alpha generated by the 9.13% distribution is computed as:

(0.0913)*(1.358)=0.1239 or about 12.39% a year in discount alpha.

This is way more than the 1.45% baseline expense ratio. As long as the HFRO managers can stabilize performance, the fund should be able to generate an attractive total return going forward.

Future Potential For Activist Activity

Institutions hold about 51.7% of the shares, which is up considerably over the last few years.

Here are some opportunistic investors that owned shares in HFRO as of March 31, 2025:

Activist # of shares

Source: Highland Opportunities and Income Fund Common Shares of Beneficial Interest (HFRO) Holding Activity, Ownership Summary, & Shareholders | Nasdaq

I would love to see Saba Capital join the party. When Boaz Weinstein was asked if he would consider an activist play on HFRO on his Twitter/X feed, he replied "Don't mess with Texas".

Bulldog Investors has reduced its holdings over the last few years, perhaps because of the tender offer. I would love to see them add considerably to their current position. Raymond James is currently the largest institutional investor by far with about 8 million shares. But these holdings most likely aggregate smaller managed accounts from many different financial advisors.

To my knowledge, there is no current activist activity occurring with HFRO. But it is nice to have activist investors on board to keep the fund managers on their toes. There is always the possibility that one or more of the activist owners could start an activist campaign in the future.

Highland Income Fund (HFRO)

  • Total investment exposure: $919 Million

  • Total Common assets: $780 Million

  • Annual Distribution (Market) Yield= 9.13%

  • Last Regular Quarterly Distribution= $0.0385 (Annual= $0.462)

  • Fund Baseline Expense ratio: 1.45%

  • Discount to NAV= -57.62%

  • Effective Leverage: 26%

  • Average Daily Volume (shares)= 234,000 (Source: Yahoo Finance)

  • Average Daily Dollar Trading Volume = $1,180,000

HFRO is a fairly liquid stock and usually trades with a bid-asked spread of one or two cents. But when the market gets slow, the spread can sometimes widen out. There is usually limited size available on both the bid and ask price of less than 1,000 shares. Because of this, I would recommend using multiple smaller orders to accumulate a position. I usually use limit orders, but occasionally use small market orders when the bid-ask spread is only a penny.

I believe that HFRO is at a decent level for a starting purchase or to add to an existing position with the discount above 53%.

data.title: Amex Exploration: Talking About 'Near-Term' Production With A Preliminary Economic Assessment
data.description:
Description

Amex Exploration (TSXV:AMX:CA) is an early-stage mineral exploration company focused on the Perron Property located on Quebec's side of the Abitibi greenstone belt. In recent weeks, Amex completed the strategic acquisition of the Perron West Property, an extensive land package directly adjacent to the Perron Property. As a result, Amex's mining claims now cover 19,752 hectares, which represents a sharp increase versus the 4,560 hectares covered before the transaction.

Under the purchase agreement, Amex will pay the seller an initial cash consideration of $150,000 on top of issuing 1.5 million shares. The seller will retain a 1.5% gross overriding royalty and be eligible for various milestone payments. Given the purchase price and the prime location in the heart of the Abitibi greenstone belt, the acquisition of Perron West makes perfect sense from a strategic point of view.

When I think about the Abitibi greenstone belt, I think about Canadian Malartic's incredible story. During my time in Toronto, in the spring of 2018 to be more precise, I remember having dinner with John Burzynski of Osisko along with other members of Toronto's Young Mining Professionals association. At the time, I was a research analyst at a small hedge fund specializing in natural resources and small-cap investing. I was learning a lot during office hours and having drinks with great people in the evenings. If I look back over my entire career, those were the best times. I also realize that spending time with inspiring people is something that I truly miss now that I have been living in the woods for three years. Of course, there is nothing more awe-inspiring than spending time in Nature, but there is also something uplifting about meeting people passionate about what they do. I feel that many professionals are sleepwalking through their careers without truly being alive. After all, it is just a big game, is it not? Why is there a need to be so serious? Let's play instead!

Anyhow, after producing ~650,000 ounces of gold in 2024, Canadian Malartic is now one of the world's largest gold mines and also one of Agnico's (AEM) crown jewel assets. From a quality point of view, Agnico's portfolio may well be unparalleled in the gold mining industry. A significant proportion of the company's economic value is underpinned by assets located in the Abitibi greenstone belt and in proximity to Amex's land package.

If Canadian Malartic is one of the world's largest gold mines based on production volume, the LaRonde Mine is also quite unique, with proven and probable gold reserves of 2.5 million ounces at an average grade of 6.36 g/t. I plan to research Agnico Eagle in the coming days, but for now, my goal is simply to highlight the prime neighbourhood in which Amex's land package is located.

In January 2024, Eldorado Gold (EGO) announced a CAD$15 million strategic investment in Amex. With a 9.9% interest, Eldorado became Amex's second-largest shareholder, just behind Eric Sprott at ~12%. Eldorado Gold is the first company I have written about after joining this platform in September 2024. The company continues to work diligently on bringing its world-class Skouries Project into production. After numerous delays and budget overruns, commercial production is now expected in mid-2026.

Even though Eldorado's portfolio is currently composed of four operating assets, the impact of Skouries will be substantial and bring free cash flow generation to a new level. The simple fact that Skouries is expected to produce an average of 140,000 ounces of gold at an average cash cost of -$365 over its 20-year mine life is enough to illustrate the point. Eldorado Gold: All Eyes On Skouries

As a reminder, Eldorado also owns the Lamaque Complex in Val d'Or, one of the largest towns within the Abitibi greenstone belt. Throughout 2024, the Lamaque Complex produced almost 200,000 ounces of gold at an average grade of 6.74 g/t. In other words, Eldorado has first-hand experience with high-grade underground mines within the belt. Knowing that it is precisely what Amex is trying to develop at Perron, the relationship between both companies starts to make sense.

Production Talks Based on PEAs

Just a few days ago, Amex published an updated mineral resource estimate on the Perron property. The technical report was based on 46,355 meters of new drilling data in addition to 562,137 meters of historical drill data. After approximately 600,000 meters of drilling, the Perron Property is now estimated to host 1.6 million ounces of measured and indicated gold at an average grade of 6.14 g/t. In terms of grade, Perron is directly comparable to LaRonde and Lamaque, so that's great. However, one of Amex's biggest problems is tonnage, and in my view, it is particularly disappointing after so much drilling and capital spending.

Throughout its life, Amex has raised approximately CAD$140 million in total. So shareholders have injected CAD$140 million, and what did they get in return? The answer is a large land package in a prime location, but with only 1.6 million ounces of M&I resources, which is hardly enough to justify building a mine.

In multiple interviews, Amex's CEO, Victor Cantore, mentioned that the company was moving towards 'near-term production'. However, I was surprised to find out that the company's plans were entirely based upon a preliminary economic assessment, or PEA. As a reminder, producing a PEA is the first step in a project's lifecycle. Generally, it is followed by a pre-feasibility study and finally by a bankable feasibility study. I wonder how the company can talk about near-term production when everything is based on preliminary economic studies. Moreover, after approximately 600,000 meters of drilling and after raising ~CAD$140 million, I am quite surprised that there is no pre-feasibility study, to say the least.

I am the first to admit that Perron's economics look great according to the most recent PEA published in November 2024. We are talking about an after-tax IRR of ~40% and a quick payback period of less than two years. It looks great, but how much is it worth knowing that it is all based on preliminary modelling?

How many remember the story of Rubicon Minerals and the Phoenix Deposit in the Red Lake camp of Ontario? Similar to Amex, the results of Rubicon's PEA were fantastic, and the company went straight to production without any pre-feasibility or feasibility studies. The results were disastrous, and Rubicon eventually filed for bankruptcy. It is only after the first gold pour was expected that the company realized Phoenix's geology was more complex than previously thought. Ironically, this is exactly the kind of information that you find out when doing pre-feasibility and feasibility studies. I am not saying that Amex is the next Rubicon Minerals, but Perron's gold mineralization does not look so simple to extract, with multiple open pits and underground workings required.

Rubicon did look like a near-perfect story: A high-grade discovery in Northern Ontario’s legendary Red Lake camp, home of the world’s richest gold mine. But this company had numerous skeptics, and they shared a concern about its strategy. Put simply, Rubicon tried to rush a challenging project into production,and it skipped important steps along the way. It went right from the PEA to construction, raising hundreds of millions along the way. What went wrong at Rubicon Minerals

Instead of talking about short-term production, why are we not talking about a pre-feasibility study in the first place? Why the rush to enter into production? Is it just a marketing technique to stir up excitement among retail investors, or do they really plan to move ahead? I don't know the answer, but I think it is worth asking. After all, it is my sole responsibility to protect my hard-earned capital. If I make a poor investment decision and lose a portion of my capital, I have nobody else to blame except myself. I could try blaming the wild creatures surrounding me, but I doubt it would lead to anything constructive.

Capital Spending

According to Amex's 2024 financial statements, travel and representation expenses totalled CAD$0.5 million throughout 2024, on top of another ~CAD$0.5 million in advertising and congress fees. It may not seem like much, but the money spent on travel and conferences does not go in the ground. When looking at Amex's travel calendar for 2025, management will visit the Bahamas, South Africa, Hong Kong, Switzerland, Sweden and the United Arab Emirates, among many other destinations. In my view, it is definitely important to maintain strong relations with the investor community, but we are talking about a mining exploration company that is still generating zero revenues.

Once consulting fees, salaries and bonuses are included, the burn rate increases to almost CAD$3.0 million per year. Based on the current market capitalization of ~CAD$180 million, it is equivalent to a 'time decay' of almost 2.0% per year. Once the capital required to fund the various drilling programs is included, it becomes clear why Amex's number of shares outstanding has increased rapidly over the last few years. As illustrated below, the number of shares outstanding has basically doubled since 2020. How many more shares will have to be issued before reaching first production, if Perron ever reaches that point?

Conclusion

Amex owns a valuable land package totalling 19,752 hectares in the heart of the Abitibi greenstone belt. In addition, approximately 600,000 meters of drilling have been completed on the Perron Property. After raising ~CAD$140 million over the years, Amex recently announced a mineral resource estimate containing 1.6 million ounces of measured and indicated gold at an average grade of 6.14 g/t. There is no doubt that some tangible value must be attributed to that. In my view, this asset base may be particularly attractive for an intermediate producer like Eldorado, for example. With rising gold prices, an increasing amount of capital could trickle down into mineral exploration.

My key issue with Amex is the concept that management talks about near-term production when the Perron Property is still in the preliminary economic assessment phase. I do not want to draw direct parallels, but it strangely reminds me of Rubicon's story with the Phoenix Deposit.

In terms of capital allocation, I also question the concept of attending so many conferences all around the world. Before investing in a junior mineral exploration company without any revenues, I would prefer the tightest cost structure realistically possible. Assuming a constant amount of drilling, the tightest cost structure translates into less dilution over time for the shareholders. Of course, the financial interests of the management team may not necessarily coincide with my own. This is the definition of 'Agency Risk', and I think it is a concept worth keeping in mind, not just for Amex, but for any publicly listed entity.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SOXQ's Performance May Be Weighted Down By The Broader Semiconductor Market
data.description:
Invesco PHLX Semiconductor ETF (NASDAQ:SOXQ) is an ETF whose strategy is designed to mirror the underlying Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX). Despite the largest holdings in the strategy being in the AI-related semiconductor space, a vast proportion of the portfolio resides in companies that cater to the less advanced semiconductor market. Given the potential market-related risks these companies hold, I am recommending SOXQ with a Hold rating.

SOX is a modified market capitalization-weighted index that is composed of semiconductor companies. This includes companies that participate in the design, distribution, manufacturing, and sale of semiconductors. The Index holds a total of 30 individual stocks, making this strategy relatively concentrated, particularly when considering the weightings of the top 10 holdings.

Because SOXQ tracks the underlying Index, the ETF is considered a passive strategy. This exposes investors entirely to the SOX Index and its respective constituents. SOXQ doesn’t add value by active weights or stock selection.

Performance in the underlying Index has been relatively lackluster, underperforming the S&P 500 (SPX) by roughly -18.52% in the last year.

Because positions are stagnant to the underlying Index, performance may not be optimized to overweight companies that are positioned for growth while underweighting companies may underperform. For example, Nvidia (NVDA), SOXQ’s top position, outperformed the ETF by 27.84% in the last year. Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), on the other hand, underperformed the ETF by -23.35% in the last year.

Investors in SOXQ also have significant exposure to semiconductor manufacturing equipment companies, which have generally experienced a challenging year, with 1-year performance significantly underperforming the SOX Index. The semicap industry has faced modest headwinds as the broader semiconductor market fell into a decline. According to Deloitte, total silicon-wafer shipments fell by roughly 2.4% in 2024 despite the market realizing revenue growth resulting from AI chips. I believe this trickled into the semi-cap market, resulting in a modest decline in machines being sold. Semi-cap companies account for roughly 17.88% of the total portfolio.

Looking to semiconductor designers, specifically for AI chips, stocks have been significantly divergent with Nvidia being the primary growth driver. In the last year, Intel Corporation (INTC) and AMD have significantly underperformed the Index, while Nvidia outperformed.

I believe this trend may continue as market share across market segments rotate, with AMD taking over Intel’s x86 market share for client computing and Nvidia taking over market share for data center servers. Though a comparison won’t be 1-for-1, I believe it generally represents the rotation of the market. For investors directly holding NVDA shares, this rotation has been beneficial. For those that are buying into portfolios that hold each of the stocks, performance may be relatively challenged. In total, these three stocks account for 20.88% of the total portfolio weight, with AMD and INTC accounting for significantly smaller proportions at 4.54% and 3.29% respectively.

Another area of growth may be in the XPU market, consisting of Broadcom (AVGO) and Marvell Technology (MRVL), each accounting for 12.33% and 2.65%, respectively.

AVGO shares have significantly outperformed the Index by a wide margin of 99% in the last year, driven by Broadcom’s key partnerships for XPU development across multiple hyperscalers. These agreements have placed Broadcom in a potentially high-growth market to develop custom XPUs and in a position to cross-sell its server interconnect technology. XPUs are custom-developed server chips that are purpose-built for their respective hyperscalers’ data centers. These chips were designed to compete with Nvidia’s GPUs on a cost-performance basis. Marvell also participates in this market but at a much smaller scale. Nonetheless, Marvell has a high-growth potential to cater to this growing market.

Lastly, semiconductor companies with heightened exposure to the smartphone industry, such as Skyworks Solutions (SWKS), Qorvo (QRVO), and Qualcomm (QCOM), have faced significant selling pressure in the last year, underperforming the semiconductor index. This cohort of companies may have been impacted by the soft smartphone market, resulting in lackluster stock performance. QRVO and SWKS were particularly impacted, with 1–2 years of declining top-line growth.

Risks Related to Holding Index Funds

Index funds are not actively managed, meaning that they are designed to reflect the underlying Index, providing investors with passive performance. As a result, it is left to the investors to actively manage their positions and risk in order to achieve their investment mandate. This compares to an actively managed strategy, where professional portfolio managers aim to beat their stated benchmark by actively managing portfolio risk, whether it’s through stock selection or active weights.

Investing in industry-specific index funds can add an additional layer of risk, given the portfolio concentration in the stated industry. Depending on the investor’s total investment allocation, there may be significant overlap in positions across portfolio strategies. For example, comparing the top 10 holdings between SOXQ and State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF SPY will show that each portfolio has an overweight position in NVDA and AVGO shares.

Given the underperformance of the semiconductor index when compared to the S&P 500, as referenced in the top chart in this report, it may be worth considering diversifying stock exposure if the concentrated semiconductor strategy is too risky of an investment to undertake. Depending on how the total portfolio is structured, it may be wise to assess overlap to ensure no additional risk is taken on. Given the equities held in SOXQ, investing in the strategy goes well beyond investing in AI-related semiconductors.

In my opinion, investing in this strategy would be looking for a turnaround in the broader semiconductor industry in which an investor must consider the state of the automotive industry, smartphone growth, consumer electronics, industrial IoT, and other end-markets for semiconductors before making an investment decision.

Conclusion

SOXQ is an industry-specific index ETF that aims to track the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index. This strategy may provide an investor industry-specific exposure to the broader semiconductor sector, including design, fabrication, and semicap equipment, amongst other corners of the industry. SOXQ has an expense ratio of 19bps, a relatively low cost for an industry-specific strategy. Given the potential headwinds faced in non-AI semiconductors and the exposure to these companies, I am recommending SOXQ with a HOLD rating. For those looking for exposure to the industry, I recommend minimizing exposure with an allocation of 1-3%.

data.title: Undervalued Gulf Operator: Talos Energy's Fundamentals Outshine Short-Term Oil Noise
data.description:
Talos Energy Inc. (NYSE:TALO) is an independent oil and gas company whose main activity is carried out in the Gulf of Mexico. Currently, Talos offers investors an attractive combination: stable cash flow, low debt, and high production growth, and projects that might boost their revenues in a few years. In 2024, the company has integrated the acquisition of Quarternorth and is now producing a record ~ ​​101 MBOE/D, from which ~ 68% is oil. In Q1 2025, Talos exceeded analysts' forecasts based on revenue, EBITDA, and free cash flow. In addition, the company insured ~42% of its oil production at a price above $72, which prevents low price scenarios. Nevertheless, the market has not yet acknowledged this growth, and the shares are traded at a discount. Talos has high leverage to oil prices, and its low cost allows it to generate profits even in a $40/BBL environment. High concentration of Gulf of Mexico projects, deep drilling, and wide-scale development (Katma, Sunspear, Daenerys) show that the company is preparing for long-term shareholder value creation. The risk consists of high dependence on oil prices, high operating costs on deep platforms, and possible regulatory changes.

Q1 2025 Financial Results

Talos Energy achieved strong financial and performance results, despite lower commodity prices. The company produced an average of 100.9 thousand barrels of oil equivalent per day (BOE/D), of which 68% consisted of crude oil. Production increase was driven largely by the QuarterNorth Energy acquisition.

Quarterly revenue reached $513.06 million, approximately +19.3% more than a year ago. The majority of revenue was oil sales – $293 million, gas – $57 million, and NGL – $73.6 million. The average realized prices were: oil – $71.73/BBL, gas – $4.32/MCF, NGL – $21.78/BBL. The price of oil is almost identical to the WTI, and gas prices were pressured by the general overproduction of the US market.

The costs increased from $373 million to $426 million, but are mainly associated with higher production. Production and operating costs were $119 million, transportation – $23 million. The company also suffered a ~$16 million loss due to changes in the market value of hedging transactions.

Net loss was $9.9 million, but Adjusted EBITDA was $363 million and a cash flow from operations of $268 million. Adjusted free cash flow was $194.5 million. CAPEX was $117.6 million, mainly related to the development of Katmai and Sunspear. The total cash balance is $203 million, and the net debt stood at $1.047 billion, which is about 2.9x annualized EBITDA. In addition, Talos bought 2.3 million of its shares for $22 million and even increased buyback authorization to $200 million.

Most importantly, the company remains financially flexible, with a strong balance, growing production, and investment in high-cost projects. Despite lower gas prices, Talos successfully optimized costs, and the decreasing CapEx will increase free cash flow in the second half of the year.

Production and Assets

Talos reached a record production level through Q1 2025 - 100.9 thousand BOE/D, of which 68% consisted of oil and 78% liquid hydrocarbons. This growth is mainly related to the successful integration of the Quarternorth acquisition and implementation of new projects such as Sunspear and Katma West #2. Most of the production comes from deep drill platforms in the US Gulf of Mexico, where Talos has operator's rights for ~ 81% of production.

The most important assets include Katma, which is currently being completed, and the first oil extraction is planned at the end of Q2 2025. The well was drilled in just one month, faster and much cheaper than expected, reaching 27,000 FT depth. Its production should last for the maximum capacity for several years. The Sunspear project has already been completed and will begin to produce 8-10 MBOE/D Q2 2025.

In addition, the company actively develops high-potential deposits such as Daenerys (~ 100-300 MBOE potential), EWING BANK 953 (8-10 MBOE/D), and Monument (up to 115 MBOE with first oil in 2026). The company holds 194 MBOE in total reserves, 77% of which are undeveloped, and the average reserves' life expectancy is ~ 6 years at the current pace of production.

The company’s 2025 plan is to reach 90-95 thousand BOE/D average production, which is already in practice. Importantly, the projects are completed quickly, from drilling to production, often just a few quarters away. This allows to respond flexibly to market signals and quickly monetize discoveries.

For Portfolio Building

At first glance, Talos is just another oil company that follows the price of raw material. However, I dug deeper and my analysis shows something much more interesting: Talos not only reacts to Brent oil prices, but it does it with delays that can be used.

I used Granger Causality tests to check: Do oil prices help to predict the return of Talos shares? The answer is yes, and it is statistically significant with a delay of 3-5 days. In other words, Brent's changes "spill" to the price of Talos not immediately, but gradually, which allows active investors to start trading earlier than the market reacts completely.

This time window holds real value. Most market participants see the correlation between oil and Talos, but few check whether this connection works in advance. My tests show that a clear signal can be seen even a few days ahead of a significant share price move. It is valuable not only for traders but also for position investors who respond to cyclical signals.

In addition, the results of regression revealed that Talos behaves not as a passive Brent "follower", but as a much stronger version of sensitivity. The model shows that when Brent rises 1%, Talos rises on average 0.8%, and this is a very significant connection. More importantly, Brent's changes alone explain a quarter of all Talos fluctuations. This means that fundamental and sentimental oil changes are reflected in the Talos much more concentrated than, say, XLE or USO ETFs.

Talos also distinguishes its volatility profile. Analysis of Garch (1,1) shows that fluctuations are not accidental here - they are clustering, i.e., they cluster. After a strong movement, another is expected. This provides an additional signal to the market participants - not only when to watch Talos, but also how long to maintain the position after the signal. With a strong Brent price impulse and knowing that volatility will follow, it allows not only to enter earlier, but also to stay longer.

Quick summary after my econometrics rant:

  • Talos returns are strongly associated with Brent Oil Price - 1% oil growth determines ~ 0.8% of the stock price.
  • Brent price changes predict Talos returns with a 2-5 day delay, giving good entries.
  • Talos moves more sensitively than ETFs, and therefore acts as an effective tactical energy tool.
  • Volatility analysis shows that after sudden movements, Talos often retains major fluctuations for several days.
  • This dynamics allows not only to enter in time, but also to strategically in a position when energy impulses predominate on the market.

Valuation

Talos Energy is currently valued in a very conservative way compared to similar energy companies. P/E is 55.6 - a number that can mislead because Talos suffered large losses due to hedging positions. However, this indicator is not suitable for an energy sector player, especially one that has major cushioning and hedging fluctuations. Instead, it is worth analyzing cash flows and the EBITDA.

Talos P/FCF reaches just 1.26 when competitors such as KRP, AAVVF, or HPK are valued at 5.26, 7.21, and 3.11, respectively. This indicates that the market does not appreciate the ability to generate sustainable cash flow. The EV/EBITDA (TTM) is also extremely low - only 1.85, when the sector average is closer to 5-7 (eg, KRP - 8.09, AAVVF - 11.18, HPK - 2.79). Even BTE, valued with a lot of debt, has a higher EV/EBITDA - 1.90.

EV/Sales reaches 1.26, which indicates that Talos generates nearly as much annual revenue as its enterprise value. It is a very attractive indicator for investors looking for value opportunities in the energy sector. Price-to-book ratio is 0.53, which means that shares are traded with ~ 47% discount from accounting value.

Risks

Despite the attractive valuation and growing production, Talos poses a number of significant risks to investors. First of all, high oil dependence. With 68% of revenue coming from oil, the results of Talos are directly dependent on WTI prices. A sudden drop in oil price, such as an OPEC+ supply increase or a global recession, can greatly affect EBITDA and cash flows. Although the company has insured ~ 42% of 2025 production for $72/BBL, the rest is vulnerable.

Second, the Talos activity is concentrated in the deep drill area - the US Gulf of Mexico. Although this region is considered safe, deep drilling is characterized by high capital costs and potential environmental risks. Any accident or borehole disaster can cause enormous legal and financial losses (eg, Deepwater Horizon precedent). The company is actively investing in development (Katma, Sunspear, Daenerys), so there is a risk of project implementation. Wells may not meet geological expectations, which would reduce returns or hinder cash flow generation.

Summary

Talos Energy remains one of the most attractive opportunities for value among the independent US oil players. Despite the strong growth of production, high cash flow levels, and efficient capital distribution, the company's shares are traded at a high discount. EV/EBITDA below 2 and P/FCF ~ 1.3 indicate that the market does not appreciate the ability of Talos to generate long-term returns. The company has a stable balance sheet, a low financial leverage, and a flexible CAPEX model, which allows it to adapt quickly to raw material prices. The hedge protection ensures the stability of the activity in 2025, and projects such as Katma, Sunspear, and Daenerys can significantly increase reserves and free cash flow generation. However, investors must assess the risks associated with raw prices and the specifics of deep drilling. In a properly managed strategy, Talos can become one of the best candidates in the entire energy sector. This is an opportunity for a position with high risk but even higher chances of returns in my opinion.

data.title: HFGO: Active And Focused Mega-Cap Growth ETF Finding Its Groove
data.description:
Investment Thesis

After a challenging start, the actively managed Hartford Large Cap Growth ETF (BATS:HFGO) seems to have finally found its groove, becoming one of the top-performing funds in the large-cap growth category over the last three years. Curious as to how, I decided to dive deeper into its managers' investment philosophy and compare it with other cheaper and more established mega-cap-heavy ETFs to see if Hartford Funds has an advantage worth pursuing. In my view, I think it at least belongs in the conversation, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

HFGO Overview

Strategy Discussion and Top Ten Holdings

According to its fact sheet, HFGO's primary objective is capital appreciation. It launched on November 21, 2021, and its active managers take a top-down macro approach "that looks for opportunities in unexpected places." However, I wouldn't take that quote literally. HFGO's top ten holdings are precisely where the best opportunities have been for the last 2+ years and include heavy weightings to the Magnificent Seven, excluding Tesla (TSLA), which is the fund's #23 position out of 48.

HFGO's fact sheet also notes that the fund has "market-cap flexibility", but don't let that fool you. There is a 93.76% correlation between a stock's weight in the fund and its market cap, so while that's a slight improvement over the 99.25% figure for its benchmark, represented by the iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF), make no mistake about it - this is an extremely top-heavy and concentrated fund, and the best way to think about it is that it attempts to filter out many of the low-performing stocks found in passive, low-cost funds.

Peer Group and Performance Snapshot

In this article, I will compare HFGO with four ETFs, selected due to their similarly high concentrations in mega-cap growth stocks, as follows:

  1. iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF)
  2. Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG)
  3. Vanguard Mega Cap Growth ETF (MGK)
  4. WisdomTree U.S. Quality Growth ETF (QGRW)

With $148 million in assets under management and a 0.59% expense ratio, HFGO is by far the least popular and the most expensive. It's also the most concentrated (49 holdings, 65.71% top ten concentration), and had the highest portfolio turnover rate last year at 41%, consistent with what you might expect from an active manager.

QGRW launched about one year after HFGO, so, unfortunately, we can't make a full performance comparison. However, we can compare HFGO with the other three ETFs, and based on the chart below, HFGO has been a disappointment. Its 16.82% total return since November 9, 2021, was roughly half of its peers.

Still, it's obvious that HFGO's poor returns to date relate solely to its performance early on. The biggest problem was its 36.69% decline in 2022, which was 3-6% worse than its peers, and from the chart above, we also see an immediate decline in the first few weeks after its launch, something that's not captured in the tables below from December 2021.

Nevertheless, HFGO's three-year annualized returns were the best at 22.10%, so I think the main takeaway is that it's an aggressive growth fund that requires a long-term mindset. You should be prepared to withstand above-average declines, and I think that comes from being ultra-concentrated and also branching out into some mid-cap companies that aren't so resilient. Even its mega-cap selections are vulnerable to declines - let's not forget Nvidia (NVDA) and Amazon (AMZN) each lost 50% in 2022.

Lastly, I just want to quickly highlight QGRW's performance since its inception on December 13, 2022. As shown below, its 98.07% total return was the best, but I'm still most interested in how these five ETFs differ fundamentally, so let's dive into their composition differences next.

HFGO Analysis

Sector Allocations and Holdings Differences

To begin, please consider the following sector allocation differences between HFGO, IWF, SCHG, MGK, and QGRW. At the bottom is a sector diversification score, which I calculated by comparing each fund's allocations against a perfectly diversified and undiversified portfolio. HFGO's 4.91/10 score is 0.08 points below the peer group average, but honestly, all these scores are low. For context, the score for the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) is 6.99/10.

Next, I've also compiled a table showing the composition differences between these ETFs for their ten largest holdings on average. As shown, HFGO overweights Nvidia and Amazon by 1.89% and 2.13% but underweights Apple (AAPL) and Tesla (TSLA) by 2.35% and 2.46%.

HFGO Fundamental Analysis

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for HFGO's top 25 holdings, which comprise 85.42% of the portfolio. Again, this is one indicator of HFGO's relatively low diversification.

Here are three takeaways to consider:

HFGO: 1.30 Five-Year Beta Is Similar To Its Peers

One concern I had was whether HFGO might be more vulnerable in the next market drawdown than its peers. However, its 1.30 five-year portfolio beta is slightly better than average, and although beta only measures market risk and not total risk, it's a signal these concerns might be overblown. In addition, I took the extra step of backtesting its current portfolio against the one in 2022, and even with Nvidia and Amazon's 50% declines, HFGO's current portfolio (rebalanced quarterly) would have only marginally trailed its benchmark, IWF (-31.80% vs. 29.31%). Therefore, I'm cautiously optimistic that its managers learned a lesson that year.

HFGO: GARP Ratios Make Sense

HFGO trades at a pricey 30.48x forward earnings, but that's not that much different from its peers, and besides, its historical three-year and one-year estimated EPS growth rates are about 4% better. When dividing each fund's estimated earnings growth rate into their forward P/E ratios, we derive the following "PEG" ratios:

  • HFGO: 30.48x / 26.36% = 1.16x
  • IWF: 28.72x / 21.82% = 1.32x
  • SCHG: 29.40x / 21.69% = 1.36x
  • MGK: 31.84x / 22.53% = 1.41x
  • QGRW: 30.73x / 22.12% = 1.39x

HFGO's 1.16x figure makes it the most attractive, so as long as large-cap growth stocks remain favored, I think it has a solid chance of continuing its success over the last three years. The earlier fundamental analysis table also shows that the components of all five ETFs delivered a 7-8% earnings surprise last quarter, which is above average. For context, the quarterly EPS surprise for the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD) was only 6.17%, so for the moment, the large-cap growth segment still seems in control.

HFGO: Mid-Cap Additions Only Marginally Impact Quality

The benefit of mega-cap funds is that quality is typically high. That's evident in the fundamental analysis table that shows HFGO's selections with a 31% weighted average ROE and net margins approaching 25%. These figures are a little less than its peers, which is something I attribute to the few mid-cap selections in the portfolio, including Formula One Group (FWONK), Natera (NTRA), Live Nation Entertainment (LYV), and DraftKings (DKNG). As shown below, profit margins are either low or in the single digits for these companies.

These companies comprise 5.02% of HFGO but only 0.24% on average for the other four ETFs, so it's enough to make a slight difference, but not one I consider to be material.

Investment Recommendation

There's a lot to like about HFGO. Its growth and value statistics are very attractive, and the fund seems less risky than it was in 2022, the source year for its poor returns since its inception. Fortunately, HFGO has bounced back and has become a top-performing large-cap growth fund over the last three years, and while I don't see any clear advantages over the lower-cost options discussed today, there also aren't any clear disadvantages. Therefore, I think the most appropriate rating for HFGO is a "hold," and I look forward to continuing the conversation in the comments below. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Monte dei Paschi di Siena: Dividend Yield Is Almost 12%, But Beware Of Risks
data.description:
Intro

Since my last article on Monte dei Paschi di Siena (OTCPK:BMDPF) approximately seven months have passed and the stock has recorded a total return of +50%, significantly outperforming the S&P 500. Given that my rating at the time was a sell, I would say that I was significantly mistaken and overly pessimistic. My investment thesis was too influenced by the bank's past, as it had been repeatedly bailed out by the government after several near-bankruptcies.

The continuous bailouts have made the Italian Ministry of Economy and Finance the majority shareholder, currently owning 11.73% of the company. This may seem like a lot, but consider that until a few years ago its stake was 64.23%. Over the years, as BMDPF grew stronger, it decided to gradually sell its stake and is likely to continue doing so.

The point is that when a company finds itself in a similar situation, the risk of investing in it is very high and not everyone has the tolerance to bear it. Buying a bank that has recently almost gone bankrupt only leads to two scenarios: either you lose everything or you make triple-digit gains in a short time. Fortunately, the latter happened with BMDPF, but it was not easy to predict given its history. Investors who added it to their portfolios during the sovereign debt crisis lost virtually everything; the same goes for those who bet on it in 2022. So, while it is true that its performance has been exceptional over the past 18 months, we cannot completely ignore its past.

It will take years and years of stability for BMDPF to finally shake off its past. The last 18 months may have marked the beginning of a new cycle, but is it a buy at the current price? Furthermore, the dividend yield is almost 12%, how sustainable is that?

Fundamental Analysis

The rise in BMDPF is also influenced by strong performance in the Italian stock market. The main index is the FTSE Mib, and looking at the total return, you can see a bullish trend that has not been seen for decades.

Beyond Italy, the same is happening in other European countries, such as Germany. Europe is outperforming the United States after a long time, and cyclical sectors such as banking are seeing the highest returns. So, basically, there is positive sentiment toward Europe, and this has brought capital inflows into Italian equities. In such an economic environment, a struggling company like BMDPF can benefit greatly, and more and more investors are betting on its recovery. In fact, looking at its capital ratios, BMDPF today seems to be in a very strong position.

CET1 is almost 20%, a huge figure that demonstrates a conservative approach by management in capital allocation. Other key ratios such as LCR and NSFR are well above the thresholds considered conservative. The former is 156%, well above the minimum threshold of 100%, while the latter is 129%, well above the minimum threshold of 100%. Based on these figures, a ‘new failure’ of BMDPF appears highly unlikely, as the bank is now well capitalized. This has also been noted by investors, with the state's stake being reduced significantly in recent years. The government has managed to find new buyers thanks to BMDPF's improving performance.

Net interest income in Q1 2025 was €543 million, down 7.50% compared to the previous quarter. This deterioration does not significantly alter its solidity, as it was widely expected. The ECB is continuously cutting rates meeting after meeting, and BMDPF can no longer demand the same variable rate as a few months ago. Interest on its loans has fallen faster than the reduction in deposit costs, resulting in a decrease in net interest income. However, there is positive news for other business areas not related to traditional banking.

Net fee and commission income increased by 8.90% compared to the previous quarter and now account for almost 40% of total revenues. The most significant performances were recorded in management/brokerage and advisory activities (+15.0% or €27.70 million compared to Q4 2024) and commercial banking activities (+2.70% or €4.80 million). More specifically, in the first section, portfolio management generated most of the increase in revenues.

The improvement in the latter and the deterioration in traditional banking activities place BMDPF on a different footing than in the past. Today's company wants to grow across the board and be a group with diversified revenues. This will also reduce the cyclicality of revenues and increase profit margins (segments such as brokerage are highly scalable). Without this improvement, BMDPF would probably not have achieved triple-digit returns.

In fact, loans to customers are down significantly compared to last year (14.70%) and compared to the previous quarter (8.30%). Fewer loans mean less interest income and therefore lower net interest income. This negative factor triggered by the gradual lowering of interest rates by the ECB was offset by expansion in other sectors.

Deposits increased by 2% compared to the previous quarter, and the company has sufficient capital to meet demand for credit. In fact, the loan-to-deposit ratio is only 83.10%, another sign of fairly conservative management.

Overall, today's BMDPF is a more solid bank that is focused on diversifying its revenues. It has a good deposit base and its capital ratios are well above regulatory limits.

Valuation and dividends

Assessing BMDPF is very complicated because we cannot rely too much on the past. At the end of 2022, its Price to Tangible Book Value was 0 and only in recent years has it begun a rapid rise.

Clearly, given that the result is influenced by the difficult past, BMDPF appears very overvalued today considering its 3Y average. But when compared with the country's leading banks (Intesa San Paolo and Unicredit), the situation is reversed. Where does the truth lie? Probably somewhere in between.

After a quick triple-digit rise, BMDPF may have reached its fair value, and I don't think it's overvalued. Compared to its peers, it's much cheaper, but we can't forget how good its performance has been so far. Another 100% in a few quarters seems unlikely, but so does a collapse, as the company seems to have found some stability. For these reasons, I believe BMDPF is a hold, certainly not a sell as in my last article.

As for the dividend, the 12% yield looks like a golden opportunity, but you can't rely on it too much. BMDPF is a bank that does not follow a specific dividend policy; it simply decides from time to time how much to issue based on how good the year has been. Today it is at 12%, but next year it could be 0% in the event of an economic slowdown.

The dividend for BMDPF is something very recent; in fact, it has not paid a dividend since 2011. More than a decade without a dividend is not ideal for investors, so my advice is to be cautious about this bank. I don't think it makes much sense to invest in it just for the dividend, as the latter is far from certain and constant. If you believe that the Italian banking sector will continue to thrive thanks to the improvement in the underlying economy, then it makes sense to invest in BMDPF. Its current valuation is not too high, but everything has to go smoothly without any problems.

Conclusion

After more than a decade of problems, BMDPF may finally be ready to start afresh and put its past behind it once and for all. The deposit base is sound, regulatory ratios are stable and public ownership is becoming less and less influential. However, the sharp rise in the share price makes me skeptical about future performance, but I could be wrong.

As for the dividend, achieving a 12% yield over a sustained period is unthinkable, which is why I would avoid it if that were the sole purpose of the purchase. BMDPF is not a dividend aristocrat and probably never will be: its dividend depends entirely on the economic cycle. The Italian banking sector is currently in a positive phase, but when the wind turns unfavorable we will see if BMDPF has really changed.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Acme United Corporation: An Exercise In Patience
data.description:
In investing, you sometimes have to be prepared to be wrong for a while. Eventually, if your thesis is correct, it will all pay off. However, during that time that you are waiting, it can be unnerving and painful. One company that has not recovered as quickly as I thought it would is Acme United Corporation (NYSE:ACU), a firm that focuses on supplying customers with first aid and medical products, as well as certain sewing and paper crafting tools. Back in October of last year, I decided to revisit the business. From the time I had initially rated the company a ‘buy’ in December of 2023 until that October date, the stock had risen by only 4.7% at a time when the S&P 500 was up 23.4%. I know that's not awful since it could have been worse. But whenever I rate a company a ‘buy’, I am making the claim that shares should outperform the broader market for the foreseeable future. And clearly, that wasn't happening.

I made the case, in that article, that shares of the company should eventually show their muster. This was based on how cheap the stock was and the fact that the business was continuing to grow revenue, profits, and cash flows. Unfortunately, the stock has pulled back by 3.5% since then. At the same time, the S&P 500 has risen another 2.2%. Some investors might be tempted to throw in the towel at that point. But in all honesty, I still think the data points toward a favorable picture for shareholders. At the end of the day, I believe that this business will deliver. And as such, I have decided to keep it rated a ‘buy’ for now.

A look at recent performance

Before we get into the most recent figures, which would cover the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year, we should first touch on how Acme United performed for all of 2024 relative to 2023. During that time, revenue for the company totaled $194.5 million. That represented an increase over the $191.5 million the business reported one year earlier. Looking at the data available, we can see that the firm experienced strength in both the US and Europe. In the US, revenue increased from $163.2 million to $166.2 million. In Europe, revenue grew from $14.4 million to $15.1 million. The only weak spot for the company involved Canada, which reported a drop from $13.9 million to $13.3 million.

According to management, the rise in the US would have been even greater had it not been for Camillus and Cuda, with sales excluding those jumping about 7% year over year. That was because of market share gains across multiple product lines that the company experienced. The growth experienced in Europe would have also been higher if not for these factors, with sales otherwise expanding by 8% because of market share gains in the office channel. The weakness in Canada was largely because of Camillus and Cuda as well, without which revenue would have risen by 1% because of strong first aid product sales that were offset somewhat by school and office products that were negatively affected by economic conditions. In case you haven't been following very closely, the Camillus and Cuda business lines were sold off by the company in November of 2023. That brought in $18.3 million in cash, with another $1.5 million paid in November of last year.

Even though revenue for the company increased, profitability did pull back. Net income fell from $17.8 million to $10 million. This was because of the $12.6 million gain that the company received last year from the sale of its Camillus and Cuda business lines. Actual operating income for the company rose from $13.2 million to $14.1 million. It's also worth noting that interest expense dropped from $3 million to $1.9 million. These aren't the only ways in which the company has been doing better. While operating cash flow dropped from $28.9 million in 2023 to $12 million last year, if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $15.9 million to $19.3 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $18.3 million to $20.2 million.

For the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, we have seen revenue growth as well. Sales inched up from $45 million to $46 million. According to management, revenue in the US increased 3% because of higher sales of first aid and medical products. Unfortunately, sales of school and office products declined. This was because of a large initial order of craft products that the company received from a major mass market retailer that took place in the first quarter of last year. So in essence, revenue last year would have been some degree lower had it not been for that order. In Europe, sales dropped a whopping 7%, with foreign currency fluctuations accounting for 3% of that decline. The constant currency sales decline of 4% can be chalked up to the fact that, in the first quarter of last year, there was a large promotion of its products that did not repeat this year. And in Canada, sales increased by 5%. Had it not been for foreign currency fluctuations, the rise would have been an astounding 12%. This was in spite of the fact that sales and school office products dropped because of soft economic conditions and was driven by strong first aid sales.

The uptick in revenue for the company brought with it a small improvement in net profits. Overall net income rose from $1.6 million to $1.7 million. Operating cash flow also improved, going from negative $10.2 million to negative $3.3 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, it did remain flat at $3.7 million. It is also worth noting that EBITDA for the company remained flat as well at $4 million.

In the absence of detailed guidance from management, and considering that we only have data for a single operating quarter so far for the year, I think it would be best to value the company based on how the firm performed in 2023 and 2024. Following this approach, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. Although the price to earnings multiple is certainly within the fair value range, at least as I see it, it does look like the firm is trading on the cheap when it comes to the other trading multiples. In the table below, I then compared the company to firms that have some similarities to it. On a price to earnings basis, I found that four of the five firms were cheaper than Acme United Corporation is. But when it came to the other profitability metrics, three of the five were cheaper.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Acme United Corporation 14.9 7.7 8.7
MillerKnoll (MLKN) 40.7 5.5 13.5
Virco Manufacturing Corporation (VIRC) 6.0 4.0 3.1
NL Industries (NL) 5.8 14.8 3.3
Steelcase (SCS) 10.3 8.1 5.1
ACCO Brands (ACCO) 10.5 2.7 45.2

Even though I have decided to keep a bullish rating on the company, I do think that one risk that needs to be factored in would be tariffs. There's a lot of drama going on there and nobody knows what the end result will be. Management mentioned tariffs multiple times when they announced results for the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. They reminded investors that they have achieved over $2 million in annual productivity savings from capital projects and from production operations recently.

One thing that I found interesting is management's view that, even if things might get tough, the firm is probably better positioned than other similar companies. Management told investors that the company has eight plants operating in the US. This helps to reduce tariff exposure. In addition to this, they expect tariffs, if they remain in place, to put ‘substantial working capital pressure’ on its competitors that don't have production here and have to import their goods. Although management did not provide any evidence for this, they did say that many of their competitors don't have balance sheets that are able to deal with that kind of shake up in the industry. But with the business having net debt of only $27.2 million, Acme United Corporation is in a good position. In fact, management said that this tariff situation might create some acquisition activities because of competitors that might face substantial pain during this time.

Some of the company’s discussion also involved changing around its supply chain. For ‘some time’, Acme United Corporation claims to have been moving production to other countries from China, with the countries that they are moving their production to including Vietnam, Thailand, and Cambodia. They are also working to continue creating efficiencies, including through the use of automation. Examples here include its facilities in Rocky Mountain and Vancouver, Washington. The firm has also shifted some of its sourcing to India, though it appears as though that might take some time to ramp up.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I would make the case that while the share price return of Acme United Corporation has been disappointing, the business continues to grow and shares are trading at levels that are attractive. Given these factors, I have no choice but to keep the firm rated a ‘buy’ for now.

data.title: Delta Air Lines: Premium Strength But Growing Turbulence Ahead
data.description:
Delta Air Lines (NYSE:DAL) may appear to be flying high on robust premium and loyalty revenues, but a closer look reveals gathering risks. Macroeconomic headwinds are pressuring its core domestic economy business, and management has responded by freezing capacity growth, a red flag for operating leverage.

At the same time, costs are climbing (fuel, labour, and non-fuel unit expenses), squeezing margins despite modest revenue gains. Delta’s recent results lean heavily on high-spending customers and its lucrative Amex partnership, raising questions about the sustainability of this revenue mix if economic conditions deteriorate. Moreover, the stock’s valuation looks stretched, trading about 50% above a fair value estimate of $32.

In this article, I argue that Delta’s risk/reward is tilted to the downside. The stock’s quant rating also flashes caution, with weak scores on valuation and growth and only moderate momentum to offset macro uncertainty. Given Delta’s economic sensitivity, execution risks in managing capacity and costs, and a lofty valuation, a Sell recommendation is warranted. I expect valuation compression as investors recalibrate expectations in the face of a potential travel slowdown.

Macroeconomic Pressures Hit Main Cabin Demand

Delta entered 2025 expecting strong growth, but broad economic uncertainty (exacerbated by trade tariffs) has stalled growth in air travel demand. This impact is most pronounced in the domestic market and specifically in Main Cabin, where both leisure and corporate travellers have pulled back.

In the first quarter, Delta saw unit revenues decline 1% y/y, reflecting softer demand and more discounted fares needed to fill seats. Management openly acknowledged the “softness in both consumer and corporate travel” in Main Cabin and noted that demand has moderated since January.

Crucially, Delta’s team does not see this as a one-quarter issue. Main Cabin softness is persisting into the spring, according to President Glen Hauenstein.

Main Cabin demand softness in both domestic and international is persisting, particularly in off-peak times.

Competitors have been flooding domestic routes with cheap fares, which has pressured Delta’s Main Cabin yields. In response, Delta withdrew its full-year 2025 guidance due to the murky outlook – a prudent move that nonetheless underscores how uncertain the demand environment has become. CEO Ed Bastian sounded a note of caution, saying 2025 is playing out differently than expected as the economy quakes.

The main risk is that a further economic slowdown or recession could significantly erode Main Cabin volumes. Delta’s core customer base has trended upmarket, but the airline still needs a healthy mass market to fill the bulk of its seats. If budget-conscious travellers stay home or downgrade to ultra-low-cost competitors, Delta’s revenue could fall short of forecasts. Management is betting that premium cabins can carry the day, but this heavy reliance on affluent travellers introduces its own vulnerability (discussed more below). For now, the domestic softness is a clear warning sign that Delta’s demand tailwinds are fading.

Flat Capacity Growth Limits Operating Leverage

One of the clearest signals of Delta’s caution is its decision to slash capacity growth plans for 2025. Originally, Delta aimed for ~3–4% capacity expansion this year, but now it will keep second-half 2025 capacity flat versus 2024. In fact, the airline plans to reduce the number of Main Cabin seats it offers, “aligning supply to demand” in the softer U.S. market. This is a sharp strategic shift - essentially sacrificing growth to protect yields and margins. While it’s a prudent move to avoid oversupplying a weakening market, it also means Delta will forgo potential revenue and scale economies that come with growth.

Flat capacity has important implications for operating leverage. Airlines have high fixed costs, so growth (up to a point) can improve unit economics by spreading costs over more seats. The reverse is also true: no growth (or cuts) can raise unit costs and hurt margins if the cost base isn’t reduced proportionally. Delta is keenly aware of this trade-off. As Bastian noted, “our largest cost and lever is capacity,” and by capping growth, they aim to “protect margins and cash flow”. The company is effectively prioritizing margin preservation over expansion, a defensive posture typical of late-cycle behaviour.

However, we should recognize that flat capacity = flat revenue potential, unless pricing magically rises. Delta itself projects Q2 2025 revenue to be anywhere from 2% lower to 2% higher year-on-year – essentially flattish.

In an inflationary world, zero growth in capacity and minimal revenue gains translate to little operating profit growth, or even contraction if costs creep up. The lack of operating leverage was evident in Q1: revenue grew 3.3%, but the operating margin was only 4–5%. Management is guiding for a better 11–14% operating margin in Q2 on the back of peak summer demand, but beyond that, they’ve abstained from giving any full-year outlook. This caution suggests Delta doesn’t have confidence that growth (either demand or capacity) will meaningfully accelerate in the back half.

The execution risk here is that Delta must precisely trim capacity without cutting into muscle. It’s telling that the airline will concentrate capacity trims in weaker regions (e.g. domestic Southeast post-summer) and dayparts, rather than uniformly across the network. Even so, flat capacity in an environment of rising costs (wages, maintenance, etc.) can lead to unit cost pressure. Unless demand surprises to the upside (an unlikely scenario given macro indicators), Delta’s decision to hold supply steady could result in under-utilized resources and lost market share to competitors who choose to grow. It’s a fine line to walk – and it reinforces our cautious stance.

Climbing Costs Are Pressuring Margins

Delta prides itself on tight cost control, but the reality is that non-fuel operating costs are rising faster than revenues. In Q1 2025, Delta’s non-fuel unit cost (CASM-ex) increased by +2.6% y/y even as unit revenues fell 1%. This divergence squeezed profitability. In fact, Delta’s operating margin for the March quarter was roughly 4–5%, essentially flat versus a year ago despite higher revenue.

This trend shows how margin expansion has stalled. Delta benefited from an 11% drop in fuel price in Q1, yet still only broke even on margin improvement.

Why?

Because labor and other costs filled the gap. Delta’s employees earned pay raises and maintenance and operations costs have climbed. In Q1, Delta paid out $1.4 billion in profit-sharing for 2024 and noted higher expenses in areas like pilot training and operational reliability initiatives. While these investments support Delta’s premium service, they add to the cost burden. CFO Dan Janki admitted that non-fuel unit cost growth, though improved sequentially, still came in above 2%. The company is now taking incremental action to manage costs, aiming for low-single-digit CASM growth going forward.

Fuel remains a wildcard. Delta enjoyed a fuel expense decline in Q1 (adjusted fuel spend down 7% y/y) due to lower oil prices. But fuel prices are volatile, and recent OPEC actions and geopolitical tensions could send jet fuel costs back up. If fuel creeps higher in the second half while Delta’s capacity is flat, the carrier will face renewed margin pressure – essentially the opposite of the Q1 scenario. Delta does have a hedge of sorts with its owned refinery, but in Q1 that was only breakeven. In short, there is no cost relief guarantee from fuel going forward.

Delta’s debt load also contributes to fixed costs. The airline ended Q1 with $19.7 billion in net debt – a sizable sum that incurs ~$720 million in annual interest expense. Delta has improved its balance sheet from the pandemic peak and even earned credit upgrades from Fitch, then by S&P and finally by Moody's. However, its net debt is four times higher than 10 years ago, while EBITDA has stayed flattish during that period.

Management plans to pay down at least $3 billion of debt in 2025, but that too, requires strong free cash flow. Any slip in earnings or cash generation could slow these deleveraging plans, leaving Delta more exposed if credit markets tighten. Higher interest rates and refinancing costs are another hidden cost that investors shouldn’t overlook, albeit Delta has most of its debt at fixed rates for now.

Premium and Loyalty Reliance: A Double-Edged Sword

One pillar of the bull case for Delta has been its success in upselling customers to premium products and monetizing its loyalty program. Indeed, Delta’s Q1 results highlight strength in premium cabins and loyalty fees even as Main Cabin languished. Premium cabin revenues rose about 7% y/y, outpacing Main Cabin significantly. Hauenstein noted that premium and loyalty revenue were both up 7%, and together with other high-margin streams (like co-branded credit card revenue and cargo) they now make up nearly 60% of Delta’s total revenue.

American Express remuneration hit a record $2.0 billion in the March quarter, +13% y/y. By focusing on higher-yield customers, Delta has reduced its reliance on the most price-sensitive flyers. In 2024, for example, premium product ticket revenue grew 8% while Main Cabin ticket revenue was flat. Delta has even retrofitted aircraft to add more premium seats, reflecting demand for upscale travel.

This premium skew is a double-edged sword. On one hand, it provides resilience: Delta’s affluent consumer base and corporate clients are less likely to cancel trips at the first sign of economic trouble. Management asserts that they “have not seen any cracks yet in the premium” demand, with record load factors in premium cabins. They even expect premium yields to widen relative to Main Cabin in the coming quarter.

Delta’s brand and product investments (new lounges, on-board service, partnerships with luxury brands) seem to be paying off in customer loyalty and pricing power. Importantly, Delta’s partnership with Amex is a cash cow: it generated $7.4 billion in cash sales in 2024, and Delta expects this to grow to $10B annually over the long term.

These funds, from selling miles and other marketing tie-ins, helped Delta weather the pandemic and provide a steady revenue stream that is untethered to daily flight operations.

On the other hand, leaning so heavily on premium and loyalty raises questions of sustainability. What if the economy weakens further? Delta’s executives insist that the premium will stay resilient even “in a recessionary environment”, arguing that customers simply use miles or other means to keep flying up front. Perhaps, but history shows even business and first-class demand is not immune in a serious downturn. Companies can cut travel budgets and force employees into coach or onto videoconferences.

Wealthy leisure travellers might downgrade from business class to economy if their portfolios or incomes take a hit. Consumer behaviour can shift quickly when belt-tightening begins. Delta is effectively saying “this time is different” – that its premium-heavy model will defy cyclical gravity. I'm skeptical.

Moreover, the SkyMiles/Amex engine, while robust now, could slow if consumer spending moderates. Delta earns rich fees from American Express largely because cardholders are charging lots of purchases and the co-branded cards are attracting new members. A broad pullback in consumer spending or a tightening credit environment (higher interest rates, lower card approval rates) could tap the brakes on co-brand revenue growth. There’s also concentration risk – Delta’s $7–10B/year Amex stream depends on a single partner. Any change in that relationship or a shift in how customers redeem miles could impact the economics.

In sum, Delta’s revenue mix has undeniably shifted to higher-margin areas, which is positive for profitability, as long as those areas keep performing. The current mix – ~60% from premium fares, loyalty, cargo and other diversified streams – is unprecedented in Delta’s history. Hauenstein even stated,

I don’t think we’ve ever had premium as a larger percent of our total revenues as we do right now…while parts of our business [mostly lower-end Main Cabin] are challenged.
